---
title: "Quiz: Solving linear equation systems"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

Short questions to test your understanding of linear systems and solving them
by hand. Try each before opening the hint or answer.

---

**Q1.** Which of these is **not** a linear equation in the unknowns
$x_1, x_2$?

(i) $2x_1 + 5x_2 = 3$  (ii) $x_1 - x_2 = 0$  (iii) $x_1 x_2 = 4$
(iv) $7x_1 = 1$

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
"Linear" means each unknown appears only to the first power, on its own — never
squared, and never multiplied by another unknown.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
(iii). The term $x_1 x_2$ is a **product of two unknowns**, which is not
linear. The others are all weighted sums of the unknowns.
:::

---

**Q2.** Write the system
$$
\begin{aligned}
4x_1 + x_2 &= 9\\
x_1 + 3x_2 &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$
in the form $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$. What are $A$ and $\mathbf{b}$?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Each equation is a row. The coefficients of $x_1, x_2$ fill $A$; the right-hand
numbers fill $\mathbf{b}$.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
:::

---

**Q3.** In the deconvolution setup $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, what does each
of $A$, $\mathbf{x}$, and $\mathbf{b}$ represent biologically?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
One holds known cell-type signatures, one the unknown amounts, one the bulk
measurements.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$A$ = the **cell-type signatures** (column $j$ = signature of cell type $j$);
$\mathbf{x}$ = the **unknown amounts** of each cell type; $\mathbf{b}$ = the
measured **bulk signals**.
:::

---

**Q4.** Solve by substitution: $x_1 + x_2 = 5$ and $x_1 - x_2 = 1$.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
From the first equation, $x_1 = 5 - x_2$. Substitute into the second.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$x_1 = 5 - x_2$, so $(5 - x_2) - x_2 = 1 \Rightarrow 5 - 2x_2 = 1 \Rightarrow
x_2 = 2$, and $x_1 = 3$. Check: $3 + 2 = 5\ \checkmark$, $3 - 2 = 1\ \checkmark$.
:::

---

**Q5.** Which of the following row operations is **not** allowed during
Gaussian elimination (because it would change the solution set)?

(i) Multiply a row by $0$.  (ii) Multiply a row by $-2$.
(iii) Add 3 times row 1 to row 2.  (iv) Swap row 1 and row 2.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
What information is lost if you multiply an entire equation by zero?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
(i). Multiplying by **zero** turns an equation into $0 = 0$, destroying its
information. The allowed scaling is by any **non-zero** number.
:::

---

**Q6.** You have reduced an augmented matrix to
$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 1 & 2 & 5 \\ 0 & 3 & 9 \end{array}\right].
$$
What are $x_1$ and $x_2$?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Read the bottom row first — it has only one unknown.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
Bottom row: $3x_2 = 9 \Rightarrow x_2 = 3$. Top row:
$x_1 + 2(3) = 5 \Rightarrow x_1 = -1$.
:::

---

**Q7.** Is $x_1 = 2,\ x_2 = 1$ a solution of
$5x_1 - x_2 = 9$ and $2x_1 + 3x_2 = 8$? Show how you check.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Substitute both values into **both** original equations and see if each holds.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
Eq 1: $5(2) - 1 = 9\ \checkmark$. Eq 2: $2(2) + 3(1) = 7 \ne 8$. The second
equation fails, so **no**, it is not a solution.
:::

---

**Q8.** To eliminate the $x_1$ term from row 2, you have rows
R1: $2x_1 + 4x_2 = 6$ and R2: $3x_1 + x_2 = 4$. What multiple of R1 do you
subtract from R2?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
You want the new $x_1$ coefficient in R2 to be zero. The multiplier is
$A_{21}/A_{11}$.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$\tfrac{3}{2}$: replace R2 by $\text{R2} - \tfrac{3}{2}\text{R1}$. The $x_1$
coefficient becomes $3 - \tfrac{3}{2}\cdot 2 = 0$.
:::

---

**Q9.** In R, what does `solve(A, b)` return, and what does `A %*% x` compute?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
One solves the system; the other is matrix multiplication used to check it.
See [`solve`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/solve.html) and
[`%*%`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matmult.html).
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
[`solve(A, b)`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/solve.html) returns the vector
$\mathbf{x}$ solving $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$.
[`A %*% x`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matmult.html) multiplies $A$ by $\mathbf{x}$;
if $\mathbf{x}$ is correct it should reproduce $\mathbf{b}$.
:::

---

**Q10.** When you build a matrix in R with
`matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 2)`, the result has $1, 2$ in the **first
column**, not the first row. What argument makes it fill by row instead, so the
code matches the equations on paper?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
See the `byrow` argument of [`matrix`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matrix.html).
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
`byrow = TRUE`. By default `matrix` fills column by column (`byrow = FALSE`),
which is a common source of mistakes when transcribing a system.
:::

---

**Q11.** A bulk sample contains 3 cell types but you measured only 2 marker
genes. Have you written enough equations to pin down all three amounts? Why?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Count equations versus unknowns. Each measured gene gives one equation; each
cell type is one unknown.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
No — 2 equations, 3 unknowns. The system is **under-determined**, so it
generally has infinitely many solutions rather than one. (Day 2 makes this
precise.)
:::
